name: Brute Force (every pair)
summary: Ignore the sorted order and try every pair of indices until one sums to the target. Simple, but O(n²) — it wastes the very property the optimal exploits.
visualizationIntent: init: the array as cells. loop (L4): pointer i fixes the first index (special). move (L5): pointer j scans every later index (current). check (L6): readout numbers[i] + numbers[j] vs target. return (L7): when a pair sums to target, both cells glow result (the answer pair, 1-indexed).

lineExplanations.3: Record the number of elements to bound both loops.
lineExplanations.4: Fix the first index i of the pair.
lineExplanations.5: Pick a second index j that comes after i.
lineExplanations.6: Check whether this pair of values sums to the target.
lineExplanations.7: It does — return the pair as 1-based indices.

syntaxExplanations.1: class Solution: — the class wrapper the platform expects your method to live inside.
syntaxExplanations.2: def twoSum(self, numbers, target): — numbers is sorted; target is the sum to reach.
syntaxExplanations.3: n = len(numbers) — the number of elements.
syntaxExplanations.4: for i in range(n): — outer loop fixes the first index.
syntaxExplanations.5: for j in range(i + 1, n): — inner loop picks a later second index.
syntaxExplanations.6: if numbers[i] + numbers[j] == target: — does this pair sum to the target?
syntaxExplanations.7: return [i + 1, j + 1] — return the pair as 1-based indices.
